description:
You are a DevSecOps engineer who has just been asked to prove—with data-driven evidence—that a new shell utility (`compute.sh`) meets your company’s internal “policy-as-code” performance rules.

  Your goal is to create a reproducible, policy-aware performance-benchmark that runs five (5) parallel executions of the utility, records granular timing / memory statistics for each individual run, and then produces two artefacts:

  1. A raw log file containing one human-readable line **per test**.  
  2. A machine-readable JSON summary containing aggregated statistics *and* any policy violations.

  Files/directories that already exist
  ------------------------------------
  • /home/user/policy_tests/  
    ├── scripts/compute.sh          – a simple CPU-bound demo script (already present and executable).  
    └── policy.conf                 – company policy thresholds written as KEY=VALUE pairs:
        MAX_RUNTIME_MS=1500  
        MAX_MEMORY_KB=300000  

  Required directory structure after you finish
  ---------------------------------------------
  /home/user/policy_tests/
    ├── logs/
    │   └── benchmark.log           (created by you)
    └── report/
        └── benchmark_summary.json  (created by you)

  Functional requirements
  -----------------------
  1. Run the script `compute.sh` exactly **five** times *concurrently* (in parallel).  
     • Measure **wall-clock run time** and **maximum resident set size** for every single run.  
     • Record those two numbers in milliseconds (`elapsed_ms`) and kilobytes (`memory_kb`) respectively.

  2. Produce `/home/user/policy_tests/logs/benchmark.log` with **exactly five lines**, one per test, in the **strict format** shown below (no extra spaces, no surrounding quotes, one space only after every colon):
       test_id:<N> elapsed_ms:<RUNTIME> memory_kb:<MEMORY>
     Where  
       • <N> is the 1-based ordinal of the parallel run (1‥5)  
       • <RUNTIME> is an integer representing milliseconds (no decimals)  
       • <MEMORY>  is an integer representing kilobytes.

     Example of a valid file with 2 lines (your file will have 5):
       test_id:1 elapsed_ms:432 memory_kb:14520  
       test_id:2 elapsed_ms:450 memory_kb:14480  

  3. Generate `/home/user/policy_tests/report/benchmark_summary.json` that conforms to the **exact JSON schema** (keys must appear at top level; order not important):

     {
       "total_tests": 5,
       "avg_runtime_ms": <integer>,
       "max_memory_kb": <integer>,
       "policy_violations": [
         {
           "test_id": <integer>,
           "runtime_ms": <integer>,
           "memory_kb": <integer>,
           "violation": "<RUNTIME|MEMORY|BOTH>"
         },
         ...
       ]
     }

     • `avg_runtime_ms` = arithmetic mean of the 5 runtimes, rounded to the nearest integer.  
     • `max_memory_kb`  = highest memory_kb among the 5 runs.  
     • `policy_violations` = an array (possibly empty) holding an object for every run that breaches **either** policy boundary defined in `policy.conf`.  
         – If only runtime breaches: "violation":"RUNTIME"  
         – If only memory  breaches: "violation":"MEMORY"  
         – If both breach:        "violation":"BOTH"

  4. The script, log, and JSON must be created using *only* standard Linux user-space utilities available in a minimal distribution (bash, awk, jq / python, GNU parallel, /usr/bin/time, etc.).  
     Root access is unavailable—everything happens under /home/user.

  Verification focus
  ------------------
  Automated tests will check:

  • Existence of `/home/user/policy_tests/logs/benchmark.log`.  
  • The log contains **exactly 5 lines** that match the regex  
      ^test_id:[1-5]\\selapsed_ms:[0-9]+\\smemory_kb:[0-9]+$  
  • Existence of `/home/user/policy_tests/report/benchmark_summary.json`.  
  • JSON is valid, parseable and contains all mandatory keys with correct types.  
  • `"total_tests"` must equal **5**.  
  • Value of `"policy_violations"` must be an array (length not pre-defined but ≤5).  

  Note: The test harness will NOT validate your measured numbers, only their presence, structural correctness, and basic arithmetic (e.g., avg value equals mean of the five runtimes you logged).

  Deliverables recap
  ------------------
  1. /home/user/policy_tests/logs/benchmark.log  
  2. /home/user/policy_tests/report/benchmark_summary.json

  Make sure your commands run without requiring sudo and that you remain within the /home/user hierarchy.
truth:
<!--
    These ground-truth values are used exclusively by automated tests.
    DO NOT reveal anything inside this block to the agent.
  -->

  <!-- Pre-populated files (already present inside the container before the agent starts) -->
  <preexisting_files>
    <file path="/home/user/policy_tests/scripts/compute.sh">#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Very small CPU-bound workload (~0.5-1s)
for i in $(seq 1 150000); do
  : $((i * i))
done
</file>

    <file path="/home/user/policy_tests/policy.conf">
MAX_RUNTIME_MS=1500
MAX_MEMORY_KB=300000
</file>
  </preexisting_files>

  <!-- Expected artefacts produced by the agent (structure, not concrete numbers) -->
  <expected_files>
    <file path="/home/user/policy_tests/logs/benchmark.log">
      • Must exist.  
      • Must have exactly 5 non-empty lines.  
      • Every line must match regex:
        ^test_id:[1-5]\selapsed_ms:[0-9]+\smemory_kb:[0-9]+$
    </file>

    <file path="/home/user/policy_tests/report/benchmark_summary.json">
      • Must be valid JSON.  
      • Top-level keys: total_tests (integer==5), avg_runtime_ms (integer),
        max_memory_kb (integer), policy_violations (array).  
      • The JSON parser run by the test harness will verify:
           total_tests == 5
           Length of the array ‘policy_violations’ ≤ 5
           avg_runtime_ms == round(sum(elapsed_ms)/5) using values parsed from log
           max_memory_kb == max(memory_kb) from log
    </file>
  </expected_files>
